coredata.dc:title: Label-free fluorimetric detection of CEA using carbon dots derived from tomato juice
coredata.dc:creator: Miao, Hong
Wang, Lan
Zhuo, Yan
Zhou, Zinan
Yang, Xiaoming
coredata.dc:description: A facile-green strategy to synthesize carbon dots (CDs) with a quantum yield (QY) of nearly 13.9% has been built up, while tomato juice served as the carbon source. Interestingly, not only the precursor of CDs and the whole synthesis procedure were environmental-friendly, but this type of CDs also exhibited multiple advantages including high fluorescent QY, excellent photostability, non-toxicity and satisfactory stability. Significantly, a label-free sensitive assay for detecting carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA) in a continuous and recyclable way has been proposed on the basis of adsorption and desorption of aptamers by the surface of CDs through a competitive mechanism. To be specific, the richness of carboxyl groups of the CDs enabled strong adsorption of ssDNA to the surface of CDs through π-π stacking interactions, resulting in the effective fluorescence quenching by forming CDs-aptamer complexes. The stronger binding affinity between CEA and CEA-aptamer than the π-π stacking interactions has been taken advantage to achieve immediate recovery of the fluorescence of CDs once CEA was introduced. Thereby, quantitative evaluation of CEA concentration in a broad range from 1ngmL−1 to 0.5ngmL−1 with the detection limit of 0.3ngmL−1 was realized in this way. This strategy can be applied in a recyclable way, broadening the sensing application of CDs with biocompatibility. Besides, the CDs were used for cell imaging, potentiating them towards diverse purposes.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
CEA
Assaying
Photoinduced electron transfer

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence quenching of CDs by CEA-aptamer
Principle of detecting CEA-aptamer and CEA
Selectivity and interference from coexisting substance
Cell cytotoxicity and confocal microscopy imaging

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We gratefully acknowledge financial support by Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities ( XDJK2015A005 and XDJK2016D033 ), Innovative Research Project for Postgraduate Students of Chongqing ( CYS14049 ). Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Schematic illustration of synthesis of CDs; (B) schematic illustration of assaying CEA by CDs.

(A) The maximum excitation and emission spectra of CDs (inset: photographs under visible light (Ⅰ) and UV light (Ⅱ) respectively); (B) HR-TEM images of CDs (insert: DLS of CDs); (C) FTIR spectrum of CDs; (D) XPS survey spectrum of CDs.

(A) FL spectra of CDs, CDs and CEA-aptamer in the presence and absence of CEA; (B) FL spectra of CDs by adding various concentrations of CEA; (C) selectivity of the proposed method by comparing CEA (5μgmL−1) with other related proteins such as AFP, CA125, CA15-3, BSA, dopamine, tyrosinase, Glucose oxidase, thrombin, (5mgmL−1 for each); (D) influence of coexisting compounds and ions on the fluorescent method by introducing Vit B2, Vit B1, ascorbic acid, norepinephrine, glucose, folic acid, bile acid, insulin, lactose, Na+, K+, Fe3+, Ca2+, respectively (5mgmL−1 for each).
Schematic illustration for assaying CEA by the designed strategy.

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of CDs with different concentrations of CEA-aptamer; (B) plot of F/F0 versus the logarithm concentrations of CEA-aptamer; (C) fluorescence spectra of CDs and CEA-aptamer with various concentrations of CEA; (D) plot of F/F0 versus concentrations of CEA from 1ngmL−1 to 500μgmL−1.

head.ce:title: Label-free fluorimetric detection of CEA using carbon dots derived from tomato juice

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Pharmaceutical Sciences, Engineer Research Center of Chongqing Pharmaceutical Process and Quality Control, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Pharmaceutical Sciences, Engineer Research Center of Chongqing Pharmaceutical Process and Quality Control, Southwest University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Medicine and Cardiovascular Institute, University of Pennsylvania, 3400 Civic Center Boulevard, Philadelphia, PA 19104, USA

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Medicine and Cardiovascular Institute, University of Pennsylvania

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A facile-green strategy to synthesize carbon dots (CDs) with a quantum yield (QY) of nearly 13.9% has been built up, while tomato juice served as the carbon source. Interestingly, not only the precursor of CDs and the whole synthesis procedure were environmental-friendly, but this type of CDs also exhibited multiple advantages including high fluorescent QY, excellent photostability, non-toxicity and satisfactory stability. Significantly, a label-free sensitive assay for detecting carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA) in a continuous and recyclable way has been proposed on the basis of adsorption and desorption of aptamers by the surface of CDs through a competitive mechanism. To be specific, the richness of carboxyl groups of the CDs enabled strong adsorption of ssDNA to the surface of CDs through π-π stacking interactions, resulting in the effective fluorescence quenching by forming CDs-aptamer complexes. The stronger binding affinity between CEA and CEA-aptamer than the π-π stacking interactions has been taken advantage to achieve immediate recovery of the fluorescence of CDs once CEA was introduced. Thereby, quantitative evaluation of CEA concentration in a broad range from 1ngmL−1 to 0.5ngmL−1 with the detection limit of 0.3ngmL−1 was realized in this way. This strategy can be applied in a recyclable way, broadening the sensing application of CDs with biocompatibility. Besides, the CDs were used for cell imaging, potentiating them towards diverse purposes.

ce:section.ce:para: Tumor markers, appearing as molecules in blood or tissue, have been proved to be associated with carcinogenesis, and their related identification or measurement exhibited great importance for patient diagnosis or clinical management (Miyake et al., 2010; Eppler et al., 2002). The determination of tumor markers plays an important role in the early diagnosis of cancer, differentiating malignant from benign conditions, evaluating the extent of disease, monitoring tumor response to therapy, and predicting recurrence (Asawatreratanakul and Thavarungkul, 2006; Fakhraddin and Padide, 2006). Carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA), a tumor-associated antigen, is expressed in many malignancies such as lung cancer, ovarian carcinoma, breast cancer, and cystadenocarcinoma (Chen et al., 2009; Bisceglie et al., 2003). Accordingly, quantitative and selective detection of CEA is critical for clinical purposes. However, amounts of CEA in colon tissue of adults are reported as a low level of 2.5–5.0ngmL−1 (Withofs et al., 2000; Zamcheck and Martin, 1981), which makes it quite a challenge to detect CEA level using available approaches. Currently, immunoassays and related techniques including enzyme immunoassay, chemiluminescence immunoassay, Raman spectroscopy, surface plasmon resonance and electrochemical immunoassay have been considered as major analytical methods towards detecting CEA (Gomez-Hens et al., 2009; Hui et al., 2009; Wang et al., 2009a,b; Bi et al., 2009; Zhong et al., 2010). Howbeit, most of these methods show drawbacks, such as requiring radiation hazards, time-consuming, poor precision and sophisticated instrumentation, leading to a strong desire for the development of alternative approaches.

Aptamers, as artificial oligonucleotides (DNA or RNA) isolated through a selection process in vitro or systematic evolution of ligands by exponential enrichment (SELEX) (Osborne and Ellington, 1997; Wilson and Szostak, 1999), can bind to a wide variety of entities with high selectivity, specificity and affinity (Ellington and Szostak, 1990; Tuerk, 1990). Compared with other natural receptors such as antibodies and enzymes, aptamers show various unapproachable advantages including easy production by chemical synthesis, convenient acquisition from commercial sources and satisfactory chemical stability (Kwame et al., 2009; Nutiu and Li, 2005). In addition, aptamers generally exhibit high specificity and affinity for their targets, and their structural flexibility allows for adaptation of undergoing significant conformational changes once aptamers and their targets specifically bind with each other (Blind and Blank, 2015; Nielsen et al., 2010). Hence, these attractive superiorities potentiate them as ideal biosensing molecules (Billinge et al., 2014; Kim et al., 2015; Ara et al., 2014; Song et al., 2014), and aptamer have been exploited in biorecognition applications of proteins, cancer cells and cancer medicine (Li et al., 2014; Xiang et al., 2015; Ghasemi et al., 2015; Kim and Gu, 2014).

Currently, various types of nanomaterials have been employed to provide fluorescence response and construct ultrasensitive biosensors with the development of nanoscience and nanotechnology (Min et al., 2011; Zhang, 2012). Fluorescent nanomaterials, especially quantum dots (QDs) and noble metal nanoclusters (NCs), have been appearing as powerful and sensitive probes and attracted numerous attentions (Yang and Lian, 2014; Wen et al., 2014). Likewise, carbon dots (CDs) appear as a new class of ‘zero-dimensional’ fluorescent nanomaterials in the carbon family (Baker and Baker, 2010), and exhibit enormous prospects across the scientific disciplines owing to their ease of synthesis, abundance of raw material in nature, excellent chemical and colloidal stability, low photobleaching, scarcely optical blinking, favorable biocompatibility and superior water dispersibility (Hola et al., 2014; Song et al., 2014; Yang et al., 2013; Dong et al., 2012; Song et al., 2012). Consequently, CDs are attracting more attentions, and becoming a promising class of nanosensors for multifunctional purposes (Guo et al., 2013; Cheng et al., 2014; Gogoi et al., 2015; Cui et al., 2015). Also, CDs have been successfully applied in fields of clinical diagnostics, food industry and environmental safety (Miranda et al., 2011). Surprisingly, the fluorescent property of carbon dots has not been exploited to develop biosensors for detecting clinically important proteins.

Herein, we report a green synthesis of carbon dots by one-step hydrothermal treatment of tomato juice under comparative conditions of low temperature (150°C) and short time (120min) ( Fig. 1(A)). The mechanism for the formation of CDs involved polymerization, dehydration and carbonization of the constituents of tomato juice such as saccharides, citric acid, and ascorbic acid (Hsu and Chang, 2012; Sun and Li, 2004). Again, both the precursor of CDs and the synthesis procedure are substantially environment-friendly, facilitating their biocompatibility and more extensive applications. More importantly, we developed a label-free fluorimetric assay with high sensitivity and selectivity for monitoring CEA level, while CDs served as the fluorescent probe and CEA-aptamer as the intermediate (Fig. 1(B)). Specifically, the fluorescence of CDs was obviously quenched once CEA-aptamer was introduced owing to the adsorption of CEA-aptamer (ssDNA) to the surface of CDs and the subsequent formation of CDs-aptamer complex. Upon the addition of CEA, the stronger binding affinity between CEA and their corresponding aptamers induced unwinding of CEA-aptamer from the CDs surface, leading to the recovery of quenched fluorescence. In the light of the adsorption and desorption of aptamers from the surface of CDs through a competitive mechanism, this proposed strategy showed satisfactory sensitivity and selectivity for detecting CEA in practical samples in a continuous and recyclable way. Besides, the CDs were used for cell imaging, potentiating them towards diverse purposes.

Tomatoes were bought from Yonghui Supermarket (Chongqing, China). CEA, AFP, CA125, CA15-3 were purchased from Boson Biotech Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). The DNA were synthesized and purified by Sangon Biotech Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China), and their corresponding sequences are as follows: 5′-ATACCAGCTTATTCAATT-3′ (CEA-aptamer). Metal ions (Fe3+, Ca2+, K+, Na+), thrombin, tyrosinase, dopamine, glucose oxidase, heparin sodium, bovine serum albumin (BSA) glucose, folic acid, norepinephrine (NE), bile acid, vitamin B2, vitamin B1, insulin, and lactose were obtained from Sangon Biotech Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Disodium hydrogen phosphate (Na2HPO4) and sodium dihydrogen phosphate (NaH2PO4), glacial acetic acid (HAc), phosphoric acid (H3PO4), boric acid (H3BO3), sodium hydroxide NaOH, sodium chloride (NaCl) were purchased from Dingguo Changsheng Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). Ultrapure water, 18.25MΩcm, produced with an Aquapro AWL-0502-P ultrapure water system (Chongqing, China) was employed for all the experiments.

All fluorescence measurements were performed on a Hitachi F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Tokyo, Japan) with excitation slit set at 5nm band pass and emission at 5nm band pass in 1cm×1cm quartz cell. Meanwhile, UV/vis absorption spectra were recorded by a Shimadzu UV-2450 spectrophotometer (Tokyo, Japan). The high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) images were produced using a TECNAI G2 F20 microscope (Portland, America) at 200kV. Elemental and functional group analyses were obtained by ESCALAB 250 X-ray photoelectron spectrometer and Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (Tokyo, Japan), respectively. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of CDs was obtained using Cu Ka radiation at a voltage of 40kV and a current of 200mA with 2h scanning mode (Tokyo, Japan). The quantum yields were obtained by using Absolute PL quantum yield spectrometer C11347 (Hamamatsu, Japan). The Raman spectrum of the as-prepared CDs on the Ag substrate was recorded with a LabRAM HR800 Laser confocal Raman spectrometer (Horiba Jobin Yvon Inc., France) at ambient temperature (about 25°C). The powder of CDs was obtained by lyophilisation in PiloFD8-4.3V (Charlotte, USA). Images of cells were taken with Olympus fluorescence microscope 1×71 (Tokyo, Japan). A Fangzhong pHS-3C digital pH meter (Chengdu, China) was applied to measure the pH values of the aqueous solutions and a vortex mixer QL-901 (Haimen, China) was used for blending the solutions. The thermostatic water bath (DF-101s) was purchased from Gongyi Instrument Co., Ltd. (Gongyi, China).

The green CDs were synthesized for the first time here. In brief, 10mL tomato juice was added into a 25mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave. After heating at 150°C for 120min, the autoclave was cooled to room temperature, and this aqueous solution was centrifuged at 3000rpm for 15min to separate the less-fluorescent deposit. Then, the aqueous solution was adjusted to neutral with 1M NaOH followed by filtered with 0.22µm filter membrane to remove the larger product. Finally, the fluorescent carbon dots were collected by dialysis against deionized water through a dialysis membrane (100 MWCO) for 24h. Again, the powder of CDs was obtained by lyophilisation, and further dissolved in ultrapure water at the final concentration of 1μgmL−1. The CDs prepared here were stable for 3 months and stored in the dark at 4°C for further applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence quenching of CDs by CEA-aptamer
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, 100μL of CDs (1mgmL−1) and 50μL of PBS (20mM, pH=7.4) were successively pipetted into a 1.5mL vial. Subsequently, 50μL of CEA-aptamer at various concentrations were added separately, and diluted to 500μL with Milli-Q purified water and vortex-mixed thoroughly. The mixture was further incubated to react at 37°C for 30min and subjected to the fluorescence measurements.

At the very beginning, 100μL of CDs (1mgmL−1), 50μL of PBS (20mM, pH=7.4) and CEA-aptamer (50μL, 50μM) were successively pipetted into a 1.5mL vial. Next, an appropriate volume of CEA working solution or sample solution was introduced, and then diluted to 500μL with Milli-Q purified water along with thorough vortexing. Finally, this mixture was remained reacting at 37°C for 30min and subjected to fluorescence measurements. Under the optimal conditions, CA125, CA15-3 and other biological species were selected to explore the selectivity of the sensing method for CEA. Finally, interferences originated from Vit B1, Vit B2, and other metal ions were investigated individually during the processes of assaying CEA.

Human serum samples from three healthy volunteers were originally collected from Southwest University Hospital (Chongqing, China). For the recovery experiments, impurities of the blood samples were removed by centrifugation (10,000rpm, 10min). Then, the supernatant was collected into a 1.5mL vial and supplemented with varying amounts of CEA (3ng, 300ng, 30μg). Finally, the serum samples were respectively subjected to this proposed approach.

To get an insight into the PL mechanism of the CDs, a series of characterizations had been successively performed. Accordingly, the CDs exhibited distinctly blue fluorescence with a peak maximum at 440nm upon 367nm excitation ( Fig. 2(A)). Other fluorescent properties of the CDs were subsequently investigated in detail. The aqueous solution of CDs emitted obvious blue fluorescence (Fig. 2(A), photographⅡ) under UV light (365nm), while appeared as transparent yellow under daylight (Fig. 2(A), photographⅠ). Meanwhile, the emission peak shifted to longer wavelength and the fluorescence intensity decreased with the excitation wavelengths varying from 350nm to 410nm (Fig. S1A), suggesting the emission of these CDs was excitation-dependent (Liu et al., 2012; Cheng et al., 2012). Besides, the as-synthesized CDs excited by 780nm laser showed the maximum upconverted emission peak at 470nm (Fig. S1B), suggesting their infrared upconversion property, likely due to anti-Stokes photoluminescence (Ho et al., 2012; Mu et al., 2013). Also, a high resolution transmission electron microscope (HR-TEM) was employed to directly observe the morphology and particle size distributions of these CDs. Specifically, the CDs obtained here existed as a majority population at the size of 3nm without aggregation emerged, depicting their satisfactory disparity. For the size distribution analysis, the diameter of CDs existed in the range of 3.0±1.3nm determined by dynamic light scattering (DLS) (Fig. 2(B)), which showed a satisfactory agreement with that of TEM. Meanwhile, the lattice spacing of 0.34nm was accompanied with the [002] fact of graphitic carbon (Fig. S2A) (Liu et al., 2014). Also, the XRD pattern of as-prepared CDs (Fig. S2B) revealed a broad diffraction peak centered at 2θ=24° (d002=0.34nm) attributed to turbostratic carbon phase similar to the graphite lattice spacing, and this was further confirmed by the selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern (Liu et al., 2014). Again, the Raman spectrum of CDs showed the G-band at 1595cm−1 and the d-band at 1351cm−1, which were attributed to sp2 and sp3 of carbons, respectively (Fig. S2C).

Next, Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FTIR) and X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS) were employed to analyze the surface groups, structure and components of the as-prepared CDs in detail. O–H, N–H, C–H, CCC, CO, CC and C–O groups were detected on the surface of CDs (Fig. 2(C)). Specifically, the absorption bands of O–H and N–H stretching vibrations appeared at 3375cm−1 with C–H and CN stretching vibrations at 2893cm−1 and 2411cm−1 respectively. Similarly, CO and CC stretching vibrations were at 1687cm−1. Simultaneously, the peak at 1446.2cm−1 was associated with C–H and N–H bending vibrations as well as the peak at 1205cm−1 for C–O bending vibrations. To elucidate the components of these CDs, XPS survey spectra were performed (Fig. 2(D)). Three major peaks of C, O and N obviously emerged in the spectrum, indicating that CDs prepared here were mainly composed of C, O and N. Moreover, there were three peaks displayed at 284.7eV, 285.7eV, 286.3eV and 288.0eV (Fig. S3), demonstrating the existence of C–C, C–N, C–O and CO on the surface of CDs, which had further confirmed the previous FTIR data. Simultaneously, the O1s spectrum (Fig. S4) exhibited two peaks at 531.3eV, 532.4eV and 531.8eV, which are attributed to CO, C–O–C and C–OH groups respectively. And three peaks at 399.5eV, 400.5eV, and 401.4eV of the N1s spectrum (Fig. S5) were attributed to the C–N–C, N–(C)3, and N–H bands, respectively. Overall, the FTIR data showed agreement with that of XPS, intensively suggesting that the CDs produced here were equipped with characteristic functional groups including –COOH, –OH, –NH2 and CO, thereby facilitating their excellent water solubility without any chemical modification.

Subsequently, a series of experiments were performed to explore the optimized conditions for synthesizing CDs. As depicted (Fig. S6A and B), the fluorescence intensities of CDs showed variations along with varying reaction times and temperatures, demonstrating that synthesis of CDs was dependent on these selected conditions. Thereby, 150°C and 120min served as the final condition towards the synthesis of CDs.

To evaluate the stability of CDs for bearing circumstances, the related experiments were performed. As revealed (Fig. S7A–C), the fluorescence intensities of CDs scarcely exhibited variation along with different concentrations of NaCl, varying pH and time, demonstrating their favorable stability. Moreover, the as-synthesized CDs could remain stable in the majority of organic solvents, and different solvents exhibited scarce effect on the fluorescence intensities of CDs (Fig. S7D).

Basically, richness of –COOH groups on the surface of carbon dots provided a suitable basis for probes assembling. CEA-aptamers were introduced to generate signals corresponding to targets, and the assembly was induced by “π-π stacking” between aptamers and CDs (Wang et al., 2013). Specifically, the aptamer (50μM) was introduced into CDs (1μgmL−1) in an aqueous solution (pH=7.4, 20mM phosphate buffer). Obviously, the introduction of aptamer (50μM) resulted in about 60% quenching of the fluorescence ( Fig. 3(A)), indicating that CEA-aptamer interacted with CDs and quenched their fluorescence. Considering the specific interactions between CEA and CEA-aptamer, we tested whether this fluorescence quenching mechanism can be employed for sensing CEA or not. Taking advantage of the previous phenomenon of fluorescence quenching, CEA was subsequently introduced into the mixture of CDs and CEA-aptamer. In contrast, CEA-aptamer got unwrapped from the CDs once CEA was added, leading to the restoration of native CDs fluorescence (Fig. 3(A)). Moreover, the addition of CEA up to 500μgmL−1 did not improve the fluorescence intensity (Fig. 3(B)).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Principle of detecting CEA-aptamer and CEA
ce:section.ce:para: Accordingly, this quenching mechanism was explored ( Fig. 4). The decrease in ξ-value from −13.3±1.9 to −18.7±1.5mV upon the addition of aptamers was observed, suggesting the effect of photoinduced electron transfer (PET) occurring during this process (Wang et al., 2009a,b). Meanwhile, the size of the CDs in their TEM image upon interacting with CDs-aptamer did not change (Fig. S8), further describing that the fluorescence quenching was caused by adsorption of CEA-aptamer on the surface of CDs rather than aggregations of CDs. Upon the addition of CEA, the stronger binding affinity between CEA and their corresponding aptamers induced unwinding of CEA-aptamer from the CDs surface, leading to the recovery of quenched fluorescence.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and interference from coexisting substance
ce:section.ce:para: To address whether the proposed strategy is specific for CEA sensing or not, eight other proteins were also investigated. Operationally, eight related proteins including AFP, CA125, CA15-3, thrombin, BSA, tyrosinase, dopamine and Glucose oxidase (5mgmL−1 for each) were tested for the optimal conditions with the presence of 5µgmL−1 CEA. By comparing the (F0−F)/F of CEA with those of other targets (F0 and F correspond to the fluorescence intensity of CDs before or after adding CEA), CEA was clearly distinguished from others (Fig. 3(C)), indicating that the current method possessed satisfactory selectivity.

To determine the possible effect originated from compounds and ions in physiological circumstances on measuring CEA (5µgmL−1), various substance such as vitamin B1, vitamin B2, ascorbic acid, norepinephrine, glucose, folic acid, bile acid, insulin, lactose, Na+, K+, Ca2+ and Fe3+ (5mgmL−1 for each) were separately introduced into current detection procedures. As Fig. 3(D) indicated, these biological compounds and ions showed scarce effect on the fluorescence intensity of the designed assay, describing its acceptable endurance for interference.

By virtue of the response of CDs to the aptamers, the fluorescence intensity of CDs (1μgmL−1) decreased in accord with increased amounts of CEA-aptamer ( Fig. 5(A)), ranging from concentrations of 5.65nM to 50μM (R2=0.989) with the detection limit of 1.88nM at a signal-to-noise ratio of 3 (Fig. 5(B)). Accordingly, the final concentration of CEA-aptamer was fixed to 50μM to obtain the maximal decrease of fluorescence and improve the sensitivity for the following detecting processes.

Again, the standard curve for CEA was subsequently established on the basis of serial concentrations of CEA (1.0, 5.0, 10, 50, 100, 500ngmL−1, 5.0, 50, 500µgmL−1). As shown in Fig. 5(C) and (D), the fluorescence intensity decreased proportionally with the amount of CEA introduced. The fluorescence intensity decrease (F/F0) versus CEA concentrations displayed a linear range from 1ngmL−1 to 500µgmL−1 (R2=0.974) together with the detection limit of 0.3ngmL−1 at a signal-to-noise ratio of 3, suggesting the sensitivity and promise of this analytical strategy. As far as we know, the linear range and the detection limit here we showed was obviously superior compared with those of previous reports (Table S1), demonstrating that the approach we proposed may match the higher requirement for assaying CEA.

For verifying the practicality of this developed assay, the standard recovery experiments were performed using human blood samples (Table S2). As listed, the recoveries of all samples were obtained as 101.3%, 98.7%, 102% respectively, proving the accuracy and reliability of this assay for blood samples. Thus, the proposed method may broaden ways for practical detections of CEA in real samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell cytotoxicity and confocal microscopy imaging
ce:section.ce:para: Benefited from their high fluorescence, resistance to the high ionic strength and high fluorescence quantum yield, CDs were further applied for cell imaging. The cytotoxicity of CDs was evaluated using human lung cancer (A549) cell lines through CKK-8 assay. The CDs scarcely imposed toxicity to cells (Fig. S9) even at high dose (3mgL−1) and long incubation time (24h), demonstrating the biocompatibility and possibility of these CDs for in vivo applications. As shown in the confocal microscope images (Fig. S10), A549 cells showed obvious fluorescence after being incubated with CDs (1mgL−1).

In summary, an innovative strategy to synthesize the blue-fluorescent carbon dots with tomato juice has been proposed for the first time. The whole synthesis procedure was simple, facile and environmental-friendly. Importantly, these CDs, rich in carboxyl groups on the surface, were employed for the quantification of CEA. Initially, CEA-aptamer adsorbed to the surface of CDs, depending on the π-π stacking interactions between CEA-aptamer and π-rich CDs, facilitated the fluorescence quenching of CDs. Then, the addition of CEA to this mixture recovered the fluorescent signal of CDs through unwinding of CEA-aptamer from the surface of CDs due to the stronger binding affinity between CEA and CEA-aptamer. This assay showed satisfactory stability and covered a broad linear range from 1ngmL−1 to 0.5mgmL−1 with the detection limit of 0.3ngmL−1. The low detection limit conferred the assay enough sensitivity to accurately evaluate the CEA levels in human serum. This contribution has provided a model of sensing applications by using label-free carbon dots for targeting DNA or disease markers, and will trigger wider applications of CDs in biological analyses and clinical determinations.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We gratefully acknowledge financial support by Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (XDJK2015A005 and XDJK2016D033), Innovative Research Project for Postgraduate Students of Chongqing (CYS14049).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.bios.2016.06.043.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material
.
